o Players are grouped based on both age and ability. For instance, a 14-year old might be placed in a bracket alongside peers from 12 to 15, with flexibility  to move a standout player into a higher group if appropriate. 

o Grouping also considers physical development to ensure fairness and  optimum training intensity. 

**6. Accommodation & Logistics** 

• **Accommodation Options:** 

o **Under-18 Campers:** 

▪ Stay with vetted host families, who are thoroughly checked through  Garda vetting and safeguarding processes. 

▪ Host families are matched based on the player’s needs (e.g., private  room or shared arrangements) and provide breakfast and dinner. 

o **Over-18 Campers:** 

▪ Typically accommodated in hotels. 

o **Alternative for ESL Students:** 

▪ Those taking part in the English program might stay in student  

residences near the campus. 

• **Additional Logistical Support:** 

o **Airport Pickup:**

▪ Arranged through the camp, often with an extra fee to cover costs like  fuel. 

▪ The process involves a pre-arrival introduction (via Zoom) between  the arriving family/player and the host family. 

o **Meal Arrangements & Dietary Considerations:** 

▪ While meals are generally provided by host families, some may  

prepare lunch similar to how they would for their own children. 

▪ Special dietary requirements are taken into account by the host  

families. 

**7. English Language & Cultural Immersion Program** 

• **ESL Partnership:** 

o The camp’s English program is conducted in collaboration with the Emerald  Cultural Institute, an established English language school with over 25 years  of experience in Ireland. 

• **Program Features:** 

o Lessons are held on a campus in South Dublin. 

o In addition to language classes, cultural workshops and excursions are  organized. 

o Excursions might include visits to historical and cultural sites such as the  National Museum, Giant’s Causeway, Cliffs of Moher, and other notable  landmarks. 

• **Supervision & Safety:** 

o Trips and cultural excursions are supervised by one or two staff members,  ensuring that students—especially under 18—are safe and well-cared for. 

**8. Medical & Safety Provisions** 

• **Staff Training:** 

o All staff members are first aid trained. 

o External personnel, including sports therapists and physiotherapists, are  also certified in first aid and emergency protocols. 

• **On-Site Medical Resources:** 

o A dedicated sports therapist based at the national campus and a  physiotherapist are available to handle any injuries or medical issues.

• **Safety Protocols:** 

o The academy maintains rigorous safeguarding standards, especially  important when dealing with minors and during off-field activities like  cultural excursions. 

**9. Success Stories & Future Directions** 

• **Pathways to Professionalism:** 

o The camp boasts numerous success stories: 

▪ **Jesse Stapleton:** Started at the camp and went on to play  

professionally in the Women’s League in England (with stints at clubs  like West Ham and Sunderland). 

▪ **Evan Ferguson:** Irish international who scored a hat-trick in the  

Premier League and moved to West Ham. 

▪ Other notable names include Emily Whelan (playing in Scotland) and  Gavin Kilkenny (with Fleetwood Town). 

o Many former camp participants have progressed into the full-time academy  program and even secured spots at colleges in the United States. 

• **Expansion of Sports Tours:** 

o In addition to the soccer camp, the discussion touched on developing sports  tour programs. 

o These tours might combine soccer training with broader travel experiences  in Europe (for example, trips to Greece or exploring other cultural  

destinations). 

**10. Q&A and Discussion Points** 

• **Vetting of Host Families:** 

o Detailed explanation of the safeguarding process for host families, including  Garda background checks and home assessments. 

• **ESL & Cultural Integration:** 

o Clarification on the English language program’s location, teaching quality,  and the cultural enrichment that accompanies it. 

• **Logistical Concerns:**

o Questions were raised about airport pickups, meal provision, dietary  accommodations, and the supervision of off-field excursions—all of which  were addressed with detailed processes. 

• **Staffing & Group Sizes:** 

o Discussion on how the camp adjusts the number of coaches and divides  groups based on numbers to maintain high-quality, personalized training. • **Future Collaborations:** 

o Riley expressed interest in exploring further sports tour opportunities that  might appeal to teenagers interested in both travel and sports. 

**Conclusion** 

The transcript provides an in-depth look at the dB Sports summer camp in Ireland. The  program is designed to offer: 

• A comprehensive, multi-component training regimen that includes on-field soccer  sessions, classroom tactical education, and tailored gym work. 

• A parallel ESL program that enriches the participants’ cultural and language  experience. 

• High-quality facilities, an experienced coaching staff, and robust logistical and  safety support. 

• Proven pathways for advancing to professional soccer and higher education. • Opportunities to expand into broader sports tour experiences.

title: Zinkerz
text:
**1. Meeting Overview** 

• **Participants & Context:** 

o The discussion features Sam Goldberg and Karly Burke, with questions from  additional attendees. 

o The primary focus is on explaining Zinkers’ services, including their summer  camp, online classes, and college counseling offerings. 

**2. Company Structure & Core Offerings** 

• **Three Main Pillars of Zinkers:** 

o **College Counseling:** 

▪ Assists students—especially international ones—with navigating the  complexities of college applications. 

▪ Emphasizes a personalized, one-on-one approach where the team  meets with both students and parents in their home environments. 

▪ Includes services like essay brainstorming, application strategy,  

extracurricular guidance, and building a well-rounded profile. 

o **Online Classes:** 

▪ A live prep platform that offers tailored instruction in subjects ranging  from early education (reading, elementary courses) to high-stakes  

test preparation (SAT, GMAT, LSAT, AP courses). 

▪ Uses a proprietary digital platform where students access class  

materials, track progress, and communicate with educators. 

o **Summer Camp (Zinkers Camp College Journey):** 

▪ A two-week intensive program designed to “wake up” both students  and their parents to the realities of college admissions. 

▪ Combines rigorous academic sessions with extracurricular activities  and campus visits. 

**3. Summer Camp Details** 

• **Purpose & Target Audience:** 

o Designed for high school juniors and seniors—students applying for college  in 2026 or 2027.

o Serves as an intensive “wake-up call” to combat procrastination and  information overload during the college application process. 

o Helps students get a realistic preview of college life through campus tours  and mock admissions exercises. 

• **Program Structure & Activities:** 

o **Academic Components:** 

▪ Over 70 hours of small group SAT preparation, with targeted  

instruction in math and verbal sections. 

▪ Students begin with a baseline mock SAT, are grouped by similar  

score ranges, and then reassessed midway to adjust groupings. 

▪ Daily sessions include timed sections, problem drilling, strategy  

discussions (e.g., optimal use of calculators), and essay workshops. 

o **Experiential Learning:** 

▪ Visits to 13 different colleges in the Boston area, including prestigious  institutions (Yale, MIT, Harvard, Brown) and smaller schools (Bentley,  Suffolk, Tufts). 

▪ An interactive mock admissions committee challenge where students  review essays, statistics, and overall profiles to decide on  

acceptances—illustrating the holistic nature of college admissions. 

o **Extracurricular & Social Components:** 

▪ Activities such as ropes courses, escape rooms, kayaking, and trips  to places like Six Flags. 

▪ Emphasis on building global citizenship: last year, students  

represented 13 different countries. 

o **Logistics:** 

▪ Camp is capped at 96 students to maintain a highly personalized  environment. 

▪ All-inclusive services cover housing (dormitory living with room 

sharing), meals, and transportation (including airport pickups and  

drop-offs). 

▪ A structured arrival day helps students adjust, especially those who  have never been away from home. 

**4. SAT Preparation and Personalized Instruction** 

• **Assessment and Grouping:**

o Each student begins with a comprehensive assessment covering math,  verbal, and even listening skills. 

o Assessments are based on actual SAT formats (mirroring College Board  standards) to ensure accuracy. 

o Students are grouped in cohorts of 6–7 based on their performance to tailor  instruction specifically to their needs. 

• **Curriculum Adaptation:** 

o The curriculum is continuously redefined on a weekly basis based on  ongoing assessments and progress. 

o Focus is on avoiding redundant lessons; if a student has mastered a topic  (e.g., systems of equations), the instruction moves quickly to more  

challenging material. 

o The approach is highly personalized, ensuring that each student’s learning  plan adapts to their evolving strengths and weaknesses. 

• **Technology Integration:** 

o Classes use a custom online platform that provides: 

▪ Real-time progress tracking for both students and parents. 

▪ Access to class schedules, materials, and billing information. 

▪ Tools such as a shared virtual whiteboard and video capabilities to  foster an interactive learning environment. 

**5. Broader Tutoring & Educational Services** 

• **Range of Subjects and Levels:** 

o Zinkers offers courses from early childhood (e.g., learn-to-read programs)  through to high school and even preparatory courses for standardized tests  (SAT, GMAT, GRE, LSAT). 

o There is a strong focus on providing supplemental courses that both  challenge high achievers and support students who are struggling in school. • **Ongoing Evaluation & Feedback:** 

o Every student participates in an initial assessment followed by regular  check-ins, progress reports, and personalized study plans. 

o After each lesson, educators send detailed progress notes and provide  opportunities for monthly parent-teacher conferences.

**6. Pricing & Package Details** 

• **Session Pricing:** 

o Tutoring sessions are priced between $95 and $130, varying by the type of  service (with higher rates for specialized test prep like GMAT, GRE, or LSAT). • **Counseling Packages:** 

o The comprehensive college counseling package is around $12,000. o These packages are designed for long-term support, starting as early as  possible in a student’s high school career, and are flexible enough to adapt  based on the student's changing needs. 

**7. Differentiators & Company Ethos** 

• **Personalization & Flexibility:** 

o Zinkers differentiates itself through a highly personalized approach— redefining curricula each week to ensure the content is directly relevant to  each student’s needs. 

o Unlike standard tutoring programs that follow a rigid, “chapter-by-chapter”  approach, Zinkers tailors every lesson based on individual assessments. • **Deep Relationship Building:** 

o The company fosters long-term relationships with families, viewing their  service as part of a “lifestyle” rather than a one-off session. 

o Educators are deeply involved with students’ academic and personal  growth, celebrating achievements and supporting them through setbacks. • **Origins and Branding:** 

o Celebrating its 10th anniversary, Zinkers was originally founded to help  international students (with founders from Panama) gain access to U.S.  higher education. 

o The name “Zinkers” is believed to be a play on “thinkers,” reflecting the  company’s emphasis on cultivating intellectual curiosity and holistic  development. 

**8. Q&A and Additional Insights** 

• **Key Questions Addressed:** 

o **Differences from Other Tutoring Programs:**

▪ Emphasis on personalization, continuous assessment, and dynamic  curriculum adjustment. 

▪ The relationship with families extends beyond the tutoring sessions;  they become ongoing partners in the student’s academic journey. 

o **Session Pricing and Service Range:** 

▪ Pricing details vary by service type. 

▪ While the immediate focus is on the camp and online classes, there is  potential to explore additional counseling services in the future. 

o **Company Origins and Continued Client Engagement:** 

▪ Zinkers prides itself on a long-term, familial relationship with clients— parents even joke about wishing for more children to continue the  relationship. 

▪ The support extends beyond academic tutoring to include life skills  and college preparation guidance. 

• **Logistics & Safety Measures:** 

o Detailed planning for student arrivals, handling of medications, and  proximity to local healthcare facilities (hospital and urgent care). 

o Special accommodations are in place for international students, including  guidelines on medical insurance. 

• **Communication & Support:** 

o The team is accessible for ongoing support via calls, video conferences, and  a dedicated support line. 

o Both students and parents have multiple opportunities to schedule  meetings, ask questions, and receive tailored advice.

title: Woodward
text:
**Woodward Summer Camps (Woodward Pennsylvania) — Camp Experts Partner Recap** 

**The quick take** 

Woodward PA is the “mothership” residential Woodward experience: a high-energy, action-sports camp built around world-class skate/bike/parkour facilities, skill progression, and a big, inclusive community vibe. Camp runs in **1-week sessions (Sun–Sat)** with a strong mix of **structured coaching + supervised open-ride time**, plus classic camp rec. 

**What Woodward is** 

● **Founded:** 1970 (50+ years) 

● **Core philosophy:** youth development through **progression** (skills + confidence + community) 

● **Why it’s different:** purpose-built parks + training tools (foam pits, resi landings, airbags), and a culture where beginners and elite athletes can be in the same ecosystem. 

**Locations (clarity for referrals)** 

Woodward has multiple US locations (PA, Copper, Tahoe, Park City), but this partnership is **focused on Woodward Pennsylvania only** for now. 

**Woodward Pennsylvania snapshot** 

● **Setting:** rural central Pennsylvania (“Amish country”), about **45 minutes from State College** 

● **Campus: 48 acres** with extensive indoor + outdoor parks, dirt tracks, and strong rec facilities

● **Capacity:** about **700 beds** (recent biggest week cited: **~360 campers**) ● **Typical camper profile: median age ~12**, ages roughly **7–17** 

● **Gender mix:** about **70/30 boys/girls** (actively growing girls’ participation and girls programming) 

**Programs offered (8 total)** 

**Core wheeled/action sports** 

● Skateboarding 

● Scooter 

● BMX Freestyle 

● BMX Racing 

● Mountain Biking *(new rollout this summer)* 

● Roller Skate *(runs for limited weeks)* 

**Action-adjacent** 

● Parkour / Freerunning 

● Creative Arts *(new rollout; blends action-sports culture with content creation, music, visual arts; stage performances + creative projects like “create-a-skate”)* 

**Beginner-friendly:** yes — all skill levels, with facilities designed for 

beginner/intermediate/advanced progression. 

**The Woodward method (how progression works)** ● **WIPS progression system:** color-coded skill scale used across campus + instruction

● Campers receive a **progression card** showing skills learned (helpful for parent-facing outcomes). 

● Woodward also brings in **elite athletes/pro riders** for instruction moments, Q&As, signings, etc. 

**A “day in the life” (high-level)** 

● **Sunday:** arrival + orientation + **skill assessment** (group placement) ● **Mon–Fri:** 

○ breakfast with cabin group 

○ **morning instruction** (coaching group) 

○ lunch 

○ **afternoon instruction + program events/contests** 

○ rec options built in 

○ dinner 

○ **evening open ride** + optional clinics/workshops + all-camp activity (campfire/s’mores, VIP athlete Q&A, etc.) 

● **End of week:** talent show + awards 

**Off-wheels + traditional camp fun (the “yin/yang”)** Woodward is action-sports centric, but not *only* that: 

● swimming pool 

● ropes course

● go-karts 

● slip ‘n slide / water slide builds 

● lake + fishing 

● trampolines 

● bouldering / kilter wall 

● arts & crafts + creative projects 

**Session options & flexibility** 

● **Most popular session length: 1 week** 

● **Multi-week discount: $200 off** when booking more than one week 

● **New 3-week “bucket” sessions:** weeks 1–3, 4–6, 7–9 (plus some full-summer campers) 

● **Half-week option: Sun–Wed** (often a great “starter” for younger campers; commonly converts to full-week) 

● **Switching programs:** campers can switch tracks (e.g., skate → BMX) **with parent confirmation** 

**Housing & supervision (notable evolution vs. older perceptions)** 

● Cabins near the skate park; double/triple bunks; counselor on each side (often + junior counselor) 

● **Younger campers (7–9 “mini campers”)** have **enhanced support**, including a **“camp parent” staff member sleeping in-cabin**

● Woodward emphasized they’ve strengthened the model by **separating coaching roles from cabin counselor roles** (more bandwidth for cabin culture + belonging) 

**Bullying/mental health supports called out explicitly** 

● ACA accredited; background checks; wellness standards 

● Added **camper support services**, head counselors, and behavior/mental health focused training 

● Anti-bullying work includes long partnership with the **Monique Burr Foundation** 

● Built more intentional community-building on Sunday/Monday to help new campers find buddies and reduce “lonely cafeteria moments” 

**Food, canteen, and spending controls** 

● **3 hot meals/day** in cafeteria + an included grill option (burgers/chicken sandwiches, etc.) 

● **Canteen + coffee shop** available via camper account 

● **Suggested spending: $100–$150** 

● Parents can **set daily spending limits** and **see itemized purchases** in the system 

● Sponsorship/beverage note: **no Red Bull to minors**; coffee shop options for kids are **decaf / non-caffeinated “refreshers”**; hydration supported by **Gatorade stations** 

● **Food allergies:** managed via health forms + labeling + staff communication 

**Travel & transportation** 

Most families drive, but options include: 

● **State College Airport** (~35 miles)

● **Newark International Airport** (~200 miles) — **travel package** includes **round-trip transport + sport equipment + bedding** (especially helpful for international campers) 

● **Bus service** from **Rockaway, NJ** (~170 miles) 

**Extra notes that may matter for certain families** 

● **Weekends / multi-week “stayover”:** quieter day with programming, check-ins, extra snacks, movie night; **free laundry** for stayover campers 

● **Equipment:** campers typically bring their own gear + **certified helmet**; camp has a **fleet** for upgrades/breakage/needs 

● **Winter:** PA is largely winterized/shut down for full residential ops; some limited weekend “open ride” experiences exist, but **summer is the full-program sweet spot** 

● **Adult camp:** they also run adult sessions (nice “dad/mom wants in on it” option) 

**Positioning / who it’s best for** 

**Great fit for:** 

● kids who love (or want to try) skate/scooter/BMX/parkour culture 

● beginners who need safe ramps + structured coaching + confidence-building ● teens who thrive with **autonomy + high-choice** within a supervised environment 

● families who want a camp where “progress is the product” (clear skill markers + confidence gains) 

**Potential concerns to pre-empt (and how Woodward addressed them):** 

● cabin culture/bullying → expanded supervision model + camper support services + anti-bullying initiatives

● canteen spending/sugar/caffeine → parent spending limits + itemized tracking + no energy drinks for minors 

● “is it only sports?” → strong rec menu + Creative Arts track + classic camp elements

title: Wilderness Adventures at Eagle's Landing Recap
text:
**Wilderness Adventure at Eagle Landing — What It Is** 

Wilderness Adventure at Eagle Landing is a **1–3 week, screen-free wilderness expedition program** where kids live and learn outdoors through backpacking, camping, and adventure activities. Trips are intentionally small (**max 12 campers**) and designed to help participants build skills, confidence, and tight-knit community—while having a seriously fun time outside. 

**The Big Purpose** 

This program is built around growth through challenge: helping kids step outside their comfort zone (into their “stretch zone”) while learning practical outdoor skills and developing as people. 

Wilderness Adventure’s foundation is the **four pillars (RAWC):** 

● **Respect** 

● **Attitude** 

● **Work Ethic** 

● **Communication** 

These themes are reinforced throughout the trip—especially during nightly campfire reflections. 

**Screen-Free by Design** 

Wilderness Adventure is **fully screen-free**: no cell phones for campers, and staff devices are for emergency use only. The goal is a true reset—nature, community, and independence. 

**What a Typical Week Looks Like** 

**Trips begin on Sundays** (arrival roughly **11am–1pm**) and typically end with **Saturday pickup** (roughly **10am–12pm**). 

On day one, campers:

● Get welcomed + have lunch 

● Receive and learn to pack their expedition gear (tent, sleeping bag, mat, backpack, etc.) ● Do icebreakers and group bonding 

● Learn **Leave No Trace** basics 

● Head into the field (depending on trip type) 

Throughout the week, campers learn: 

● Cooking meals on camp stoves (age-adjusted complexity) 

● Food storage + nightly **bear hangs** 

● Fire building + nightly campfire culture 

● Outdoor living routines + teamwork 

At the end of each session: 

● Campers participate in **“WA Olympics”** (fun competitions + reflection/learning tie-ins) ● Enjoy a celebratory meal (spaghetti dinner was mentioned) 

● Head home the next morning 

**Core Trip Structure** 

Every trip has: 

● **Max 12 campers** 

● Typically **2 leaders** (sometimes 3 leaders or an additional LIT depending on trip difficulty/logistics) 

● A focus on **community-building + reflection** alongside adventure programming

**Campfire Culture** 

Campfires happen **every night**—even in rain (they’ll do a “Nalgene fire” using a headlamp under a water bottle if needed). 

Campfire time includes: 

1\. **Fun + games** (decompress after the day) 

2\. **Learning discussion** (RAWC themes or Leave No Trace) 

3\. **Reflection/debrief** (processing fear, wins, challenges, group support) This is a key part of why kids return home noticeably more capable and confident. 

**Age Progression** 

Wilderness Adventure is designed as a progression where skills and independence build year to year: 

**Ages 9–10** 

● Intro to camping (lighter structure, less backpack-heavy) 

● More base-camp style living 

**Ages 11–12** 

● Introduced to backpacks + living out of them 

● Still not moving campsites nightly as a rule 

**Ages 13+** 

● More physically demanding and mobile trips

● Backpacking where campers may **set up/tear down camp nightly** and move through the National Forest 

● Mountain biking and other advanced activities begin at this age range 

**Trip Types** 

**Classic Expeditions** 

“Best of Wilderness Adventure” — a wide variety of activities. 

**Specialty Trips** 

Focused themes like: 

● **Go With the Flow** (water-based) 

● **Outdoor Engineering** (STEM + outdoor skills) 

● **Rock climbing / mountain biking emphasis** 

● **All-girls trip** option 

**Homestead Adventure** 

The “bridge” for campers/parents nervous about backcountry living: 

● **Lodge-based** (sleep in real beds) 

● Dining hall style structure 

● Still adventure-forward, but with more comfort 

**Wilderness Leadership Course (WLC)** 

The most immersive option: 

● **3 weeks**

● Includes major experiences like New River Gorge activities (whitewater rafting + rock climbing were mentioned) 

● Campers get leadership roles like “leader of the day,” helping make decisions (pacing, meals, planning, etc.) 

● They reported a **big increase in WLC participation last year** 

**Safety and Medical Readiness** 

Wilderness Adventure emphasized a strong safety culture, including: 

● All trip leaders are **Wilderness First Aid (WFA)** certified 

● Admin team often has **Wilderness First Responder (WFR)** 

● Staff training includes: 

○ risk management 

○ youth behavior coaching 

○ problem-solving and group dynamics 

● Emergency communication equipment: 

○ **Garmin inReach Mini** devices (used successfully during an incident last summer) 

**Neurodivergent Campers** 

They noted increased experience with neurodivergence over the last ~3 years, but were clear: 

● This is **not** positioned as a specialized program for high-support needs (they explicitly said it’s not a fit for high-level autism support needs) 

● Fit depends on the camper and support needs

● They want a **direct conversation with families** to understand what’s needed and whether the trip structure is appropriate 

● They mentioned challenges like **overstimulation** and needing appropriate separation/support strategies when needed 

**Where Campers Come From** 

Predominantly: 

● **Virginia** (including Roanoke, Richmond) 

● **DC area + surrounding states** 

They also get campers from across the U.S. and occasionally internationally (examples mentioned included **Israel** and **France**). 

**Arrival, Travel Support, and Pickups** 

Most families **drop off and pick up at base camp**. 

They also offer paid pickups/drop-offs from: 

● Roanoke–Blacksburg Regional Airport 

● Roanoke Amtrak station 

● Roanoke Greyhound station 

Cost: **$150 each way** (so **$300 round trip**). 

**Medications** 

Medication handling is structured and logged:

● Parents hand medications to the team at drop-off 

● Trip leaders use a **medication dispensing log** 

● Campers and leaders both **initial** when meds are administered 

● Meds are typically handled around meal times (morning/lunch/dinner) 

**Quick Placement Guidance** 

● **Outdoor Discovery (9–10):** first-time campers, early confidence building 

● **Homestead Adventure:** great “safety net” for families anxious about full backcountry living 

● **Classic Expeditions:** best for kids who want variety and broad exposure 

● **Outdoor Engineering:** strong fit for STEM-curious, hands-on learners who still want adventure 

● **Wilderness Leadership Course:** best for teens ready for a big leadership growth leap (3-week immersive)

title: Where There Be Dragons Recap
text:
**Program Offerings** 

**1. Summer Programs** 

a. **Duration**: 2, 3, 4, or 6 weeks. 

b. **Target Age**: High school students (14+ years with at least one year of high  school completed). 

c. **New Addition**: 2-week programs to accommodate shorter schedules,  primarily language-focused. 

**2. Gap Year Semesters** 

a. **Duration**: 3 months. 

b. **Target Age**: 17–22 years old. 

c. **Audience**: Students taking a gap year, graduating high school early, or  pausing college. 

**Core Program Features** 

1\. **Small Groups**: Maximum of 12 students with 3 instructors for mentorship and  safety. 

2\. **Program Components**: 

a. Homestays (rural and urban for language and cultural immersion). b. Independent Study Projects (e.g., crafts, trade skills). 

c. Trekking (varies from short day hikes to multi-week treks). 

d. Focus on cultural traditions, religious practices, and community  engagement. 

3\. **Ethical and Responsible Travel**: Prioritize supporting local economies, light  environmental footprints, and reciprocal relationships with communities. 

**Locations** 

1\. **Asia**: Nepal, Indonesia, China, Southeast Asia. 

2\. **Latin America**: Guatemala, Bolivia, Peru (recent pause on Mexico for safety  reasons). 

3\. **Africa**: Senegal and Morocco (recently added Spain to Morocco programs).

**Safety and Risk Management** 

• **Protocols**: 

o All instructors are Wilderness First Aid certified. 

o Partnership with International SOS for 24/7 medical advice and emergency  assistance. 

o Assessment of transportation and location safety by local experts and risk  management teams. 

o Flexible itineraries to avoid risky areas (e.g., Peru during protests or skipping  Mexico’s Chiapas region). 

• **Emergency Support**: 

o Instructors can step out to focus on an individual student while maintaining  group safety. 

o Regular assessments and adjustments for program safety. 

**Program Differentiators** 

• **Custom Crafted Programs**: Designed fresh each year by instructors with local  expertise. 

• **Unplugged Experience**: Programs are phone-free, fostering present-mindedness  and deeper engagement. Students have limited access to communication devices  for weekly updates. 

**Student Characteristics** 

• **Successful Students**: 

o Curious, open-minded, independent, and eager to engage in meaningful  travel. 

o Comfortable in group settings and excited for cultural immersion. • **Challenges for Some Students**: 

o Struggle with homesickness or reliance on phones. 

o Difficulty adapting to rugged travel conditions.

**Popular Programs** 

1\. **Summer**: 

a. Language-intensive programs in Guatemala, Bolivia, China, and Taiwan. b. New Japan program focusing on language, history, and arts. 2. **Gap Year Semesters**: 

a. Nepal and South America are perennial favorites. 

b. Senegal and Japan are new additions. 

3\. **Unique Offerings**: 

a. Morocco now includes a Spain component. 

b. Peru offers Wilderness First Aid Certification. 

**Student Outcomes** 

• Personal growth, cultural competency, leadership skills, and life skills. • Enhanced college readiness through self-exploration and independence. • Opportunities to earn college credit via University of Montana for students 17+ on 4- week or longer programs. 

**Enrollment Process** 

1\. **Application**: 20-minute process, requiring a deposit of $850 to reserve a spot. 2. **Program Availability**: Displayed on the website, with frequent updates on capacity. 

**Support and Resources** 

• Students are chaperoned for flights during summer programs (inbound flights); gap  year students travel independently with guidance. 

• WTBD offers consistent support via trained instructors and a central team available  for inquiries. 

**Questions and Closing Notes** 

• **Future Plans**: New programs are driven by instructor expertise in potential regions.

• **Contact Information**: Alexandria shared her email for follow-ups and inquiries. 

The presentation concluded with gratitude and encouragement for educators and families  to consider WTBD for students seeking meaningful travel experiences. 

[Note: This document also contains an expanded, more detailed ChatGPT-generated restatement of the same Where There Be Dragons (WTBD) presentation — covering speaker background (Alexandria Biddle, Outreach Manager), WTBD mission and 1993 origins, summer and gap-year program durations and ages, key program features (small group sizes of max 12 with 3 instructors, homestays, independent study projects, trekking, cultural/religious studies, ethical travel), locations across Asia/Latin America/Africa plus new Japan and fall semesters in Senegal/Japan/India, comprehensive safety/risk-management protocols (Wilderness First Aid, International SOS, flexible itineraries avoiding Chiapas/Peru protests), custom-crafted and phone-free unplugged programs, highly qualified instructors, student profiles, outcomes (personal growth, cultural competency, language skills, optional college credit, Wilderness First Aid certification in Peru), enrollment steps (~20-minute application + $850 deposit), popular programs, future plans, and contact details — Alexandria Biddle (alex@wheretherebedragons.com).]

title: Westcoast (Zoom - Nov. 4, 2025)
text:
**Westcoast Connection — Camp Experts Briefing** Summary for Advisors | November 07, 2025 

**Quick take** 

• Strong demand for Europe (especially Greece), Japan, Australia and Hawaii. Shorter programs and later July departures are trending. 

• Middle school is a growth engine: 22 separate middle school departures this season (up from 11 last year). 

• New academic and career offerings anchor the summer: Sports Business Boot Camp (NYC area), UC San Diego Collegewise accelerator, and expanded tech/finance programs. 

**What’s new for 2026** 

• Sports Business Boot Camp (partner: Prof. Steven Master, NYU). Heavy executive access (ESPN, NBA, NFL, USTA, soccer). Enrollments route through WCC — not via the professor’s site for summer dates. 

• UC San Diego expansion (Olympics prep related to UCLA). New on-campus Collegewise College Admissions Accelerator: middle school eligible (current 6th–8th). Formats: 7-day (wk 1), 7-day (wk 2), 14-day core, optional 5-day Los Angeles extension (total 19 days). 

• Middle school teen tours: European Escape (21 or 28 days) mirrors popular 9th–10th itineraries. Oceania Global Adventure adds Australia only (15 days) or Australia + Hawaii (21 days). • Short programs: Greek Islands continues; new 8-day Amalfi Coast + Rome; new 8-day Athens + Paris; Costa Rica Surf & Adventure (8 days, based in Puerto Carrillo). 

• Domestic: New York + Boston + Chicago city sampler (public transit; approx. 3 days in each city). Whistler Adventure (10 days). New Colorado + Utah Global Adventure (14 days). 

**Coming soon (not yet on site)** 

• Wall Street expansions abroad: Tokyo (digital assets, fintech, AI) and London (global markets and international investing). 

• Technology tracks: Seattle Tech (video game design; cyberdefender + AI) and Montreal Technology (AI/machine learning; video game coding). 

• Marine Biology & Wildlife Conservation (Southern California, based in San Diego) modeled after the Florida Keys program. 

**Trends & early indicators** 

• Shorter experiences and later departures: 46 departures start after July 15, enabling flexible, mix-and-match summers. 

• Europe demand is “on fire” — Greece boosts any itinerary. Japan is surging (multiple departures targeted at 26 students each). 

• Alumni savings ended Oct 31; season outlook is optimistic across Europe and Oceania portfolios. 

**Logistics to know (helpful with families)** 

• Enrollment: $750 deposit; families can cancel or switch programs without a fee until Feb 1 (switching subject to availability).

• Technology policy: phones allowed but restricted during classes, service, group meals, and structured activities. Programs use the Remind app for daily schedules/updates. No fully tech-free option at present. 

• Supervision differences: middle school enrichment has tighter structure and buddying. Daily flow includes two morning courses (approx. 1h15 each), lunch, FITs (elective-style blocks), student-choice activities, dinner, and evening events. 

• UCSD vs. UCLA: UCLA hosts the largest number of summer programs nationally; UCSD is modern with new residence clusters (Sixth and Eighth Colleges) and strong dining — a high-quality alternative when UCLA capacity is constrained. 

• Commuter option is available on many pre-college/learn programs; local students can attend academics only, though many choose residential for the social experience. 

**Advisor tools you can use** 

• Live Advisor Guide (shared sheet): tabs by grade; filters by start date, return date, and price. Columns include direct links to program pages and “click-to-enroll” for specific departures. Link remains the same each season and is kept current by operations. 

**Program fits to recommend** 

**Profile Recommend** 

Active/adventure seeker Whistler Adventure (10d); Australia or Australia + Hawaii (15/21d); Colorado + Utah GShort, high-impact Europe Greek Islands; 8d Amalfi + Rome; 8d Athens + Paris; European Escape (21/28d for miAcademic/career builder Sports Business Boot Camp; Wall Street (NYC) and upcoming Tokyo/London variants;Later departures needed Leverage the 46 departures after July 15 to stack with camps or family plans. 

**Fast answers from the call** 

• Boot Camp sign-ups: must enroll via WCC during summer (direct sign-up on partner site is for one- or two-day off-season clinics only). 

• London Markets cohort mix: roughly 25–50% North American, remainder European/international (Wall Street in NYC is ~90% American). 

• Availability pacing: multiple Europe programs expected to waitlist by late November if trends hold. 

**Contacts** 

Primary: Ira Solomon and Mitch Lerner (Westcoast Connection). For Camp Experts routing and coordination, loop Sam Goldberg and Riley McDonough as needed. 

Prepared for the Camp Experts team. This recap is based on the Westcoast Connection advisor briefing transcript.

title: WeHaKee_Camp_for_Girls_Recap
text:
WeHaKee Camp for Girls — Team Recap (Overview, Program & FAQs) 

Leadership & Transition 

Presenter: Stacie Simpson (incoming Co‑Director with husband Corey Harrison). Current directors Maggie & Bob Braun will lead their “grand finale summer” next season before retiring. Owners (including Andy from North Star Camp) remain in the background; Stacie & Corey will operate camp going forward. Stacie focuses on new camper recruitment; Maggie/Bob are managing returning families and alumni during the transition. 

Location & History 

Winter, Wisconsin (Northwoods). Property history includes a long‑running football camp by Notre Dame’s Newt Rockne, a period as Camp Chikigami (Jewish girls camp), and a St. Louis Hawks (now Atlanta Hawks) basketball camp. The WeHaKee girls’ program dates back to the 1920s near Green Bay and moved to the current site in the 1960s. Formerly operated by Dominican Sisters; privately purchased in 2021. Now secular with light traditional influences (e.g., graces before meals; some legacy songs). 

Who They Serve & Session Options 

Girls ages 7–17. Sessions: 2‑week, 4‑week, or 6‑week (a 6‑week season partitioned into 2‑week blocks; campers can combine). Leadership Academy (ages 15–17): CIT1 → CIT2 → Junior Counselor, with peer housing plus leadership/service time with younger campers. 

Cabins: 8 girls + 2 adult counselors (18+). Overall capacity ~108 campers at a time; last summer averaged ~85 (trending up). Mix by duration (last summer): vast majority two‑weekers (~180), ≈15 four‑weekers, ≈3 six‑weekers. 

Program Philosophy & Daily Rhythm 

“Light skill‑based”: emphasis on fun, trying new things, and community over competition or measurable skill jumps. Daily anchors around breakfast/lunch/dinner; optional early‑morning activities; scheduled ‘classes’ (may be re‑termed ‘clubs’) chosen before arrival; evening programs alternate between all‑camp events (e.g., capture the flag, scavenger hunt) and cabin nights (themed parties, spa nights). Ceremonial campfires (welcome, mid‑session, closing). Regular reflective ‘Evening Gathering’ in the chapel building (non‑denominational). Unstructured “Valley Time” for hanging out, card games, friendship bracelets, camp store, snacks. 

Activities Snapshot 

Creative: arts & crafts, pottery/ceramics, painting, sketching, newspaper club, instrumental & vocal music, musical theater/drama, jewelry making, crocheting, knitting, cooking.

Active: horseback riding*, archery, gymnastics/tumbling, soccer, badminton, basketball, tennis, dance, general sports. 

Waterfront (lake + pool): water skiing*, wakeboarding, swimming, canoeing, kayaking, fun‑yaks, windsurfing, sailing, fishing. 

*Extra‑fee activities: water skiing and horseback riding. Showcase Day at end of each 2‑week session highlights what campers did (friendly scrimmages/performances; low emphasis on scoring/competition). 

Facilities, Health & Inclusion 

Cabins have electricity; bathrooms/showers are in three nearby shower houses serving thirteen cabins (clean, modern; some inevitable woodland “spiders”). 

Medical: rotating on‑site coverage (typically RNs; sometimes MD or advanced wilderness‑certified provider). Food: buffet style with clear allergen labeling; separate line for dietary needs (e.g., vegan/vegetarian, no red meat); kitchen will consult on complex cases. 

Neurodiversity: prepared to support Level 1 autism/ADHD; Level 2 likely with family consult; Level 3 typically beyond camp resources—families guided to specialized programs. 

Staffing 

Approx. 40 seasonal staff for 108 beds (~⅓ via international exchange). Two counselors live in each cabin; counselors also lead activities. Additional activity specialists and seasonal leadership/admin live in staff housing. Male staff do not serve as cabin counselors; adult men may work in maintenance/kitchen roles with limited camper interaction. ~15 staff already committed early for next summer; strong return rate. 

Transportation, Laundry & Trips 

Transportation: bus pickups from the Chicago corridor and through Wisconsin; airport bus pickup at Minneapolis–St. Paul (MSP) for fliers. Laundry: commonly used by 4‑ and 6‑week campers; optional for 2‑weekers (packing light recommended due to limited cabin storage). Overnights: outdoor‑skills class may culminate in an overnight trip (varies by interest/staffing). Stacie aims to expand nature/outdoor‑living elements over time. 

Community Vibe & Extras 

Overall tone is friendly, creative, and a bit more laid‑back than highly competitive camps. Performers (theater/music) often bring extra energy, balancing the community. Family Camp: open to anyone (late summer); families get their own cabin; activity blocks offered; signup sheets for horseback riding and water skiing. Mother–Daughter Camp follows Family Camp with a girls/women‑only model. 

Enrollment & Accreditation

Enrollment is trending ahead of last summer; goal is to fill beds. Pricing uses tiered ‘early bird’ windows (increases scheduled through the season); request current sheet for exact dates. ACA Accreditation: currently accredited; reaccreditation visit slated for the coming summer. 

Key Contacts 

Primary contact for new families: **Stacie Simpson (Director)**. Owners remain supportive but not day‑to‑day. Referrals welcomed, including from Chicago/MN corridors and growing out‑of‑region markets (FL, AZ, CA, Denver).

title: Tripp Lake
text:
**1. Opening and Introductions** 

• **Meeting Kick-Off:** 

o Sam Goldberg opens the session, confirming that the recording is in  progress and inviting participants to join the discussion. 

o Katie Milne and Liz introduce themselves as the co-directors of Trip Lake.  They express enthusiasm about the meeting, their excitement to share  updates, and the desire to deepen the existing partnership with current  families and prospective campers. 

• **Purpose of the Meeting:** 

o The session is designed to explain changes in program identity and structure,  detail the evolving camp experience, and discuss the type of campers and  families that fit Trip Lake’s philosophy. 

o They mention sharing updated documents (PDFs) with information that can  be circulated to teams who might not be present. 

**2. Detailed Overview of the Daily Schedule and Program  Structure** 

**Daily Organization and Age Grouping** 

• **Structured Yet Flexible:** 

o The camp organizes campers into **seven unique age groups** that largely  determine their daily schedule. 

o Although the day is primarily spent within age-specific groups (which fosters  strong bonds and cohesion within each group and the individual bunk), there  are periods designed to mix campers across ages. 

**Elective (Sign-Up) Periods** 

• **Elective Activities:** 

o A key feature is the “sign up” system (Trip Lake’s term for what many camps  call “electives”).

o Campers generally have **2–3 elective periods per day**, allowing them to  choose from a variety of activities such as art shops, waterfront activities,  gymnastics, dance, culinary sessions, zip lining, and archery. 

o For the youngest age groups (second and third graders), there is only one  elective per day. This limited option ensures they experience a rotation of  activities (e.g., art, dance, culinary, and water-based activities) so they can  try everything in a structured rotation. 

**Open Choice Period and Evening Activities** 

• **Choice and Chill:** 

o At the end of the day, there is an open “choice and chill” period. Here,  campers have the freedom to decide their next activity—whether that’s  playing tennis, swimming, reading, or participating in specialty activities (like  improv sessions or juggling demonstrations). 

o This period is designed to blend freedom with structured choice, giving  campers the chance to self-direct while still having access to a curated  selection of experiences. 

• **Evening Programming:** 

o A unique and traditional highlight is the **theater program**. Every age group  puts on their own musical or show. 

o The theater, which is Trip Lake’s oldest building, is not just a performance  space but a place where campers develop stage confidence and learn team  collaboration (with roles ranging from on-stage performance to behind-the scenes set and technical work). 

o Even if campers choose to simply be part of the chorus or help with set  construction, the activity reinforces camp traditions and helps build a  shared identity. 

**3. Cross-Age Interaction and Community Building** 

• **Daytime vs. Evening Interactions:** 

o Although daytime activities are mostly segregated by age to ensure safety  and developmentally appropriate programming, the **evening sessions and  choice periods mix all age groups**.

o Examples include all-camp campfires, inter-age games (such as a joint  Bingo tournament or a mixed-age kickball game), and “Magic Hour” when  campers gather around the camp circle to wind down together. 

• **Living Arrangements:** 

o A notable feature is that all campers live on one large “circle.” Even if each  bunk is age-specific, the centralized living space facilitates spontaneous  interactions and long-lasting friendships, with children of different ages  mingling during downtime and in communal spaces. 

**4. In-Depth Look at Sports, Skill Grouping, and Electives** **Sports Programming** 

• **Accessibility and Flexibility:** 

o Traditional concepts such as rigid “A-team/B-team” divisions have been set  aside. Instead, sports are made accessible to all age groups, with  

modifications based on developmental stage. 

o For instance, the ropes course is integrated into every bunk’s schedule.  Young campers experience a full day split between low and modified high  ropes (with some high-rope elements accessible only after a certain age or  grade level), while older campers have the option to sign up for advanced  activities like the zip line. 

• **Team Formation:** 

o From the first week, campers are assigned to **color-coded teams** (using fun  descriptors like “smileys,” “jollies,” “cheeries,” and “laughers”) that carry  through many sports activities, ensuring that whether a camper wants to  play or cheer, they are part of a consistent team identity. 

o During team sports such as soccer, basketball, and lacrosse, the experience  is designed so that participation is flexible. Campers may choose to be  active players or enthusiastic cheerleaders, and the programming is  adjusted so that every camper finds a comfortable role. 

**Elective Skill-Level Grouping** 

• **Skill-Specific Grouping:**

o For sports like tennis and swim, activities are organized by skill level within  the larger age group. Beginners are grouped together so that they can learn  at a comfortable pace, while more advanced campers are given  

opportunities to further develop their skills. 

o This division ensures that each camper receives instruction and challenge  tailored to their ability. 

**5. Special Schedule Features and Additional Programs** **Unique Elements in the Schedule** 

• **“Kitchen Permission” and Special Treats:** 

o The schedule includes designated elements like “kitchen permission,”  where campers get to make cinnamon toast in the kitchen—a small but  cherished tradition. 

• **Tuesday Trips and Overnight Experiences:** 

o Every Tuesday, the camp organizes trips that allow campers to explore  different parts of Maine. 

o For older campers (from around 5th grade upward), there are opportunities  for multi-night trips that involve camping, hiking, mountain biking,  

whitewater rafting, and other outdoor adventures. These trips help build  independence and introduce campers to new experiences. 

**Facility Upgrades and Projects** 

• **Theater Enhancements:** 

o The theater, a central hub for evening activities and musicals, will have air  conditioning for the first time, addressing comfort issues during hot summer  days. 

• **Gymnastics and Recreation Facilities:** 

o There is an ongoing multi-year project focused on upgrading the gymnastics  facility. This space, along with the “Cliff House,” is set to be transformed into  an indoor recreation center that will broaden the scope of indoor sports and  other activities. 

• **Other Facility Improvements:**

o Air conditioning has already been installed in the dining hall and the main  office/lodge (which also houses the health center). 

o Discussions are underway about adding fans to the bunks to further enhance  camper comfort. 

**6. Enrollment, Demographics, and Community Identity** **Enrollment and Capacity** 

• **Current Numbers and Growth Goals:** 

o Last summer, Trip Lake hosted approximately 260 campers. The target is to  grow toward a capacity of 300 while maintaining a close-knit community  feel. 

o The camp’s flexible layout allows for adjustments in age groups and capacity  while preserving the intimate atmosphere. 

**Camper Demographics** 

• **Diverse Geographic Representation:** 

o Although New York remains a major source of campers, Trip Lake attracts  families from across the U.S. (including California, Florida, Chicago, Boston,  Connecticut, Denver, Houston, and Atlanta). 

o The camp also welcomes international campers (with noted participation  from Spain, France, and other countries). Arrangements are made to  accommodate short-season campers—often younger children—for those  families who cannot commit to a full seven-week session. 

**Community and Tradition** 

• **Tradition and Legacy:** 

o Trip Lake has a storied history dating back to 1911. Its longstanding  traditions—such as the cookie line, team singsongs, and signature camp  songs—serve to connect generations. 

o The camp emphasizes the “Spirit of Trip Lake” (also referred to as the Spirit  of 888), which focuses on core values like kindness, respect, courage, and  responsibility. This spirit is instilled from day one during orientation and  celebrated through awards and daily interactions.

• **Alumni Impact:** 

o Approximately 40% of campers are returning alumni. Many families, even if  first-timers, are drawn by the reputation of former campers and the promise  of long-lasting friendships (with alumni often remaining part of the extended  camp family through milestones like weddings). 

**7. Leadership Style, Parental Engagement, and  Communication** 

**Leadership Involvement** 

• **Hands-On Approach:** 

o Katie Milne and Liz are very active on campus. They join campers in daily  activities—from swimming and tennis to playful pranks—demonstrating a  leadership style that is both approachable and engaging. 

o Their presence is not limited to structured sessions; they are visible during  “Magic Hour” and informal gatherings, reinforcing the camp’s commitment  to a fun, inclusive environment. 

**Parental Partnership** 

• **Open Communication:** 

o The leadership stresses that parents are considered partners in the camp  experience. They are encouraged to communicate openly, provide feedback,  and participate in the journey. 

o Regular updates are delivered via newsletters and a recently introduced  podcast, which features recordings of camp life (including the sounds of  Cookie Line and insights from staff) to keep families connected throughout  the summer. 

• **Tours and Orientation:** 

o Camp tours and a “rookie day” are organized for prospective campers and  their families, allowing them to experience a full day at Trip Lake and meet  key staff members like Liz and Katie.

**8. Q&A and Final Remarks** 

**Questions Addressed** 

• **Inter-Age Activities:** 

o Clarification that while scheduled activities are mostly age-specific, many  sessions (especially during the open “choice and chill” period and evening  programming) are mixed, allowing for valuable inter-age interactions. • **Sports and Skill Grouping:** 

o Discussion on how sports have evolved from rigid team assignments to  flexible groupings based on both age and ability, ensuring every camper can  participate meaningfully. 

• **Facilities and Upgrades:** 

o Further details on improvements to the theater (with the addition of air  conditioning) and updates to the gymnastics facility, as well as  

enhancements in other parts of the camp (dining hall, lodge, potential bunk  fans). 

• **Enrollment Options and Additional Costs:** 

o Explanation that while the full seven-week session is common, shorter  sessions are available for younger campers. 

o Noted that additional programs—like horseback riding and certain overnight  trips—may incur extra fees, but most camp experiences are offered on an  all-inclusive basis. 

**Closing Comments** 

• **Gratitude and Future Vision:** 

o Sam Goldberg, along with Liz and Katie, expresses sincere thanks for the  partnership and the opportunity to share their vision. 

o The session ends on an upbeat note, with the directors inviting continued  questions, sharing additional documents for reference, and expressing  excitement about the camp’s future growth and community-building efforts.

title: Thunderbird (MN)
text:
• **Camp Identity and Philosophy:** 

o The camp is described as a traditional sleepaway experience offering both  3½‑week and full 7‑week sessions. 

o It is known for its classic camp traditions while providing new opportunities  for older campers. 

o The core goal is to help kids grow physically, emotionally, and socially— fostering a journey from competence to confidence. 

o The philosophy emphasizes that small successes (like water skiing for the  first time or conquering a climbing wall) build overall self-confidence and  resilience. 

o The camp values learning through challenge and even frustration, teaching  kids that moments of difficulty are opportunities for growth. 

**2. Program Structure and Experiential Learning** 

• **Traditional Experience & Multi-Session Model:** 

o Camp sessions are designed for a wide age range, starting as early as 8–10  years old, and extend into young adulthood with evolving experiences. o The program is built on a “choose-your-own-adventure” model that allows  campers to select from 20+ daily activities. 

• **Expedition and Adventure Programs:** 

o For older campers (typically 14+), the camp offers customized expedition  experiences. 

o Examples include: 

▪ A 21‑day backcountry canoe trip on a river in Canada, where campers  must qualify by building necessary physical and technical skills. 

▪ A Pacific Northwest program where campers travel to areas like the  North Cascades, Olympic Mountains, and even experience day hikes  on Mt. Rainier. 

▪ Western States expeditions in Canada and a customizable “CIT trip”  (which can range from East Coast adventures to trips in Alaska). 

o These trips require preparatory training (shorter backpacking or canoe trips)  and include elements like community service (e.g., trail maintenance  projects) to build responsibility and teamwork. 

• **Daily Choices and Adaptive Learning:**

o Each day, campers choose activities based on their interests and skill levels.  For example, if a camper is initially hesitant about the climbing wall, they set  a goal to try it, and once achieved, they might aim to climb higher or master  the technique. 

o Certain high-skill activities (sailing, horseback riding, water skiing, archery)  offer levels or “cards” that track progress and award achievements. 

o The elective-style structure means no two days are alike, and the curriculum  adapts to each camper’s progress and evolving interests. 

**3. Leadership, Staffing, and Community Integration** 

• **Leadership Structure:** 

o The camp’s leadership is tiered to ensure safety, personalized attention, and  effective program delivery. 

o At the cabin level, each group of 6–10 campers is supported by a team of  three counselors, ensuring a 3:1 ratio. 

o Each cabin is part of a larger “village” overseen by a head counselor (village  director), and further supported by assistant directors and a broader  leadership team (program director, special program director, waterfront/trip  directors, etc.). 

o A dedicated expedition coordinator oversees all adventure programs, and  specialized staff (e.g., ski boat drivers, horseback riding instructors, climbing  coaches) are rigorously vetted through interviews and required references. • **Staff Selection and Training:** 

o Staff applications (domestic and international) are carefully reviewed, and  interviews are conducted by the directors or assistant directors. 

o For specialized programs, staff must demonstrate not only technical  expertise (often with certifications like whitewater training or climbing  instructor credentials) but also the ability to adapt lessons to the campers’  skill levels. 

o Staff training begins well before camp starts (late May) to ensure that cabin  teams are built based on individual strengths and camper needs. 

o Directors emphasize that they maintain a hands-on presence on campus— whether driving the ski boat, riding horses, or simply interacting with  campers—to build trust and community. 

• **Community and Parent Engagement:**

o The camp fosters a “Thunderbird community” where relationships are built  over time. 

o Directors personally meet most new campers and their families, hosting  home visits and family meetings to integrate everyone into the camp culture. o Co-ed events and sibling reunions are scheduled (e.g., special talent shows,  4th-of-July gatherings, and themed specialty days) to ensure that campers  from both boys’ and girls’ camps interact in a supportive, low-pressure  environment. 

o Campers often develop lifelong friendships and even marry later on—a  testament to the strong community bonds. 

**4. Operational Logistics and Adaptive Programming** 

• **Daily Management and Tracking:** 

o Camp activities are managed via sign-up sheets that are entered into a  centralized database, tracking which campers attend each activity at any  given hour. 

o Staff receive detailed rosters to ensure every camper is accounted for; if  someone is missing from an activity, procedures are in place to locate them  quickly (e.g., checking the health center or contacting the program director). • **Adaptive Lesson Plans:** 

o For activities where skill-building is essential, lesson plans are adapted  based on each camper’s progress. 

o For example, in water skiing or sailing, there are designated sessions for  beginners, intermediate, and advanced participants, and staff often  

encourage peer teaching as campers become more proficient. 

o In cases where a camper is not engaged—say, a kid choosing to sit out of  basketball—staff are trained to offer alternatives, check in on the camper’s  wellbeing, and suggest other options (such as rest, a brief break, or  

switching activities). 

• **Technology and Communication:** 

o Parents are kept in the loop regarding camp progress, including daily  updates and the use of systems (like “inner camp mail”) to facilitate  communication among campers.

o Promotional material and additional program details (for new initiatives such  as a potential day camp or “rookie” program) are shared with interested  parties. 

• **Transportation and Logistics:** 

o The camp manages transportation meticulously. For example, a portion of  staff is dedicated to airport pickups (primarily in Minneapolis), with badges  and coordinated procedures to ensure smooth transitions. 

o In larger cities, staff and parent volunteers work together to oversee flights  and bus transfers, ensuring that groups of campers travel together safely. • **Medical and Allergy Accommodations:** 

o Each camp location has two nurses (at least one Registered Nurse) on site  along with a rotating doctor who stays for a week at a time. 

o The camp is located approximately 15 minutes from a hospital (with access  to a specialized children’s hospital in Fargo if needed) and maintains  protocols for emergencies. 

o Food allergies are rigorously managed. The camp offers allergen-safe menus  (for peanuts, gluten, etc.) and provides vegetarian options, while also  accommodating kosher requests when feasible. 

• **Disability Accommodations:** 

o While the camp successfully integrates many kids with ADHD and mild  autism spectrum conditions through tailored instruction, its physical  facilities (such as the lack of wheelchair ramps) may not be optimal for  campers with significant physical disabilities. 

o Each situation is considered on a case-by-case basis, with the goal of  ensuring a safe and supportive environment for all campers. 

**5. Expedition Programs in Detail** 

• **Overview of Expeditions:** 

o Expeditions are highlighted as one of the most unique aspects of the camp  experience, especially for older campers (8th grade and above). 

o These programs are designed not only as adventures but also as milestones  that require campers to qualify by demonstrating both hard (physical skills,  technical competence) and soft (teamwork, decision-making) skills. 

• **Types of Expeditions:**

o **21**‑**Day Backcountry Trip:** Campers participate in a rigorous canoeing  expedition on a Canadian river. Preparation includes training sessions and  practice trips (like whitewater school) to ensure they’re ready for the  challenge. 

o **Pacific Northwest Expedition:** Campers travel from the camp (flying to  Seattle or Portland) and spend about five weeks in the backcountry.  Destinations include the Hoh Rainforest, North Cascades, and day hikes in  the Olympic region. 

o **Western States and Canada Trip:** In this experience, campers visit  destinations such as Jackson Hole, Grand Teton, and engage in service  projects (e.g., trail and campsite maintenance, building a bridge) to instill a  sense of responsibility and community service. 

o **Customized Adventures:** 

▪ For those not ready for the most intensive expeditions, shorter  programs (3‑, 5‑, or 7‑day trips) are available under an “Adventure  Quest” label. 

▪ These options allow campers to sample the expedition experience  without committing to the full intensity, and they often include  

activities like biking trips or day hikes. 

• **Skill Building and Progression:** 

o Before participating in major expeditions, campers must complete  prerequisite trips (such as a 3‑ or 7‑day backpacking or canoeing trip) to  ensure they have the necessary skills. 

o Campers set personal goals (e.g., mastering a climbing wall or learning the  basics of boat rigging) and progress through levels that are tracked via  standardized “cards” or achievement awards. 

o Peer learning is encouraged—older, more experienced campers often help  mentor younger ones, reinforcing their skills and confidence. 

• **Safety and Staffing for Expeditions:** 

o Expeditions are coordinated by a full‑time staff member dedicated to  planning and logistics. 

o Specialized training (wilderness first responder, whitewater certification,  etc.) is required for staff who lead these trips. 

o Throughout each expedition, the camp ensures that campers remain within  their established groups, reinforcing the sense of community and safety. • **Communication and Home Connection:** 

o For older campers on expedition, weekly calls home are arranged, helping  maintain family connections and reinforcing the camp’s supportive network.

**6. Final Logistics, Demographics, and Wrap-Up** 

• **Enrollment and Session Mix:** 

o The camp hosts roughly 180 boys and 180 girls in their respective camps. o In each camp session, approximately 120 campers participate, with a mix of  full‑season and half‑session (either first or second session) campers. o Younger villages (grades 2–4) tend to have primarily half‑session campers,  while older groups see a higher proportion of full‑season participants. • **Transportation and Arrival:** 

o A significant percentage of campers fly into Minneapolis and are met by  designated staff. 

o Organized airport procedures ensure that groups of campers and  accompanying parents or staff remain together throughout travel. 

• **Diversity and Community Integration:** 

o The camp intentionally avoids clustering campers from the same school or  area, encouraging a mix of backgrounds (with representation from across the  United States and internationally, including families from Europe, Brazil,  Mexico, Israel, the UK, and more). 

o This approach helps campers reinvent themselves away from familiar school  dynamics and fosters a new “Thunderbird community” that is diverse and  dynamic. 

• **Optional Services and Additional Programs:** 

o The camp is non-denominational, though approximately 65–70% of its  campers are Jewish. A non‑religious service is held on Sunday mornings, and  accommodations can be made for families wishing to continue their  religious practices. 

o Food preferences and allergies (peanuts, gluten, kosher needs) are  accommodated. 

o The camp is also piloting a day camp or “rookie” program for younger  children (grades 1–4) as a soft launch, offering a shorter experience for  families not ready for the full session. 

• **Closing and Promotional Material:** 

o As the meeting winds down, Shari and Daniel mention that additional  promotional materials—featuring photos, videos, and detailed descriptions  of both Girls Camp and Boys Camp—will be sent to interested parties.

o The session ends with thanks and an invitation for further contact if  additional questions arise.

title: The_Road_Less_Traveled_Recap_fixed
text:
The Road Less Traveled — Team Recap (Review, 2026 Plans, Trips) 

Why Families Choose RLT 

Intentional, thoughtful programming aimed at growth (confidence, independence, leadership, language). Service with substance (students understand impact). Tech‑free cohorts accelerate bonding. Best‑in‑class communication (arrival photo, leader updates, proactive outreach). Personal touches (leaders’ end‑of‑trip letters; precise re‑match for returners). 

Core Model & Safety 

Four pillars: Wilderness Adventure, Impactful Service, Cultural Immersion, Meaningful Travel. Ages: completing 6th–12th grade. Group size: typically 14 (MS 12; some HS to 16) with 3 leaders. Leaders age 21+ (avg 23–25), WFR, lifeguards; ~2 weeks in‑person training. 24/7 on‑call physician; strong in‑country partners; emotional well‑being prioritized. Escorted international flights: NYC→Europe, Miami→C/S America, SFO→Asia; UM gate meet/hand‑off. 

2026 Focus & Trends 

Maintain quality, expand awareness; add destinations for multi‑year returners. Parent demand: unplugging + healthy stretch; culture & service over pure ruggedness. 

Portfolio Snapshot 

Middle School: California (camping; env. service), Colorado (animal service; camping), Puerto Rico (house stay; adventure), Maine (1‑week taster), Costa Rica (community‑based service; Spanish immersion), France & Italy Alps (env. service; camp→house). 

High School: Hawaii (camping), Alaska (camp/cabin), Colorado Mission Wolf (rugged; 30+ years), Yellowstone (env. service), Puerto Rico Scuba (house), Italy Scuba (camp+hotels), Costa Rica (community & Spanish), Thailand 21d (elephants; teach English; hotels), Maine 

1‑wk, Vietnam & Cambodia 21d (env.+community; some camping), Japan (NEW; dates TBA), Morocco (rave reviews; desert; high‑impact service), Iceland & Norway (true backpacking; everything on your back), Greece (kayak/island‑hop; beach camping), Spain (NGOs; city+nature), Azores (donkey sanctuary; hot springs/surf), Scotland & Ireland (new; nature + city), Peru (Andes NGO; girls’ edu; Machu Picchu), Dominican Republic (public‑health‑oriented service + youth work). 

Enrollment & Balance 

Typical cap 14 (MS 12; some HS 16). Aim to reserve ~1/3 of spots for a single gender; priority is to run with min ~10. If a session skews, RLT contacts families early to switch sessions/programs.

What’s Hot / Early Movers 

France & Italy Alps (HS) Session 2 ~3 spots left. Japan (new) drawing strong interest. Morocco, Peru, Azores, DR trending on testimonials and ‘resume value’. 

Referral/Contact: info@theroadlesstraveled.com (Alicia + team). Direct: alicia@theroadlesstraveled.com; olly@theroadlesstraveled.com

title: The Nature Place Recap
text:
**About The Nature Place Day Camp** 

**History & Philosophy** 

• **40 years of operation** as a **nature-focused, non-competitive summer camp**. • Located in **Chestnut Ridge, Rockland County, NY**, approximately: o **20 minutes from the George Washington Bridge** 

o **10 minutes from the Tappan Zee Bridge** 

• Situated on a **200-acre campus** with **woods, streams, and fields**. • The camp’s **home base** is also a **private school**, allowing access to **indoor spaces**,  though the camp primarily operates outdoors. 

**Core Values** 

• **Nature-Oriented**: 

o All activities incorporate **natural elements**, even traditional camp activities  like **art, drama, and cooking**. 

• **Non-Competitive**: 

o The camp does not play **team sports**. 

o Instead, **cooperative games** foster collaboration and group problem solving. 

o The camp removes **social pressures** that exist in school and traditional  competitive camps. 

• **Socially Nourishing**: 

o Encourages a **break from school-year stress**. 

o Builds a **supportive, inclusive community** where campers can **develop  friendships without external pressures**. 

**Camp Structure & Enrollment** 

**Session Length & Commitment** 

• **6-week camp session** runs from **June 30 – August 8**. 

• Families can enroll for **4, 5, or 6 weeks**, with a **4-week minimum**. • **Four-week enrollment conditions:**

o Campers **must start in the first or second week**. 

o **First two weeks must be consecutive** to foster group cohesion. o No **2-week enrollment** to maintain **strong group dynamics** and **ensure a  positive camper experience**. 

**Camper Grouping** 

• Campers are grouped based on **age and developmental level**, **not strictly by  grade**. 

• Example: A group of **6.5 – 7.5-year-olds** may include 1st and 2nd graders. • **Mixed-gender groups** with **16 campers per group (±1-2 campers)**. • **Younger campers (ages 4-5)** have **smaller group sizes** for better support. 

**Staffing & Training** 

• **High staff-to-camper ratio**: **100 staff for 250 campers**. 

• **No high school students** are hired. 

• Each group is led by **three staff members**: 

o **Two college-aged counselors** (typically in their undergraduate years). o **One post-college head counselor**, often a **teacher, social worker, or  advanced-degree student**. 

• **Comprehensive staff training**: 

o Staff undergo **40+ hours of pre-camp training**. 

o Training ensures a **safe, enriching, and well-supported experience** for all  campers. 

**Daily Schedule & Activities** 

**Typical Daily Structure** 

• **6 activity periods daily**, including **one swim period** (instructional + free swim). • **10-minute transition times** prevent rushing and allow for social bonding. • **Lunch period is extended** to ensure proper nourishment. 

• **Younger campers** (ages 4-7) have an **additional snack/rest period**.

**Swimming Program** 

• **The Pond**: A **chlorinated, filtered, natural-looking pool**. 

• Swim lessons emphasize **building comfort in water first**, rather than strict  technique. 

• **Counselors swim with campers** to increase engagement. 

• Older campers can **train to become lifeguards**. 

**Lunch & Food Policy** 

• **Campers bring their own lunch** to accommodate dietary needs. • Partnership with a **natural foods café** offers **optional daily meal ordering**. • **Peanut butter is allowed**; allergy concerns are managed by **seating arrangements  and handwashing policies**. 

• **Lunch is picnic-style**, outdoors, to reinforce connection with nature. 

**Program Areas & Activities** 

**Creative & Nature-Based Activities** 

1\. **Earth Art** – Uses natural materials; some projects remain in the forest. 2. **Organic Cooking** – 

a. Campers **grind their own flour**, **harvest vegetables**, and **prepare meals** from scratch. 

b. A **camp cookbook** is sent home at the end of summer. 

3\. **Gardening & Farming** – 

a. Campers engage in planting, harvesting, and composting. 

b. A **working dairy farm on campus** provides hands-on farm experiences. 4. **Drama & Music** – 

a. **Outdoor performances**. 

b. **African drumming & dance** for **cultural exchange**. 

**Adventure & Outdoor Skills** 

1\. **Outdoor Skills** – Fire-building, knot-tying, compass navigation. 

2\. **Mucking** –

a. **Campers walk through streams**, observing **aquatic life**. 

b. Considered the **most popular activity** across all ages. 

3\. **Cooperative Games** – 

a. High-energy, **team-building games** (no competition). 

b. Some activities include **yoga and mindfulness**. 

4\. **Archery** – Available for campers **ages 8+**. 

**High Adventure & Camping Progression** 

1\. **Climbing Tree** – Real **rock climbing experience** using a **living tree**. 2. **Low Ropes & High Ropes** – 

a. **Problem-solving activities** that encourage **teamwork**. 

b. **Giant’s Ladder** requires **paired teamwork**. 

3\. **Day Hikes** – 

a. **Offsite hiking trips** in **Harriman State Park**. 

b. Hike intensity increases **with age**. 

4\. **Camping Progression**: 

a. **Almost Overnight (ages 5-8)** – Campfire, dinner, and activities (pick-up at 7  PM). 

b. **Onsite Camping (ages 9+)** – Sleeping in tents on campus. 

c. **Offsite Camping (ages 10+)** – Multi-day **canoe trips and backpacking trips**. d. **Oldest campers** go on a **2-day rock climbing expedition** in the  

Shawangunks. 

**Special Considerations** 

**Weather & Rainy Days** 

• **Rain = Liquid Sunshine!** Campers embrace rain through **puddle-jumping and  outdoor play**. 

• **Thunderstorms & severe weather**: 

o Groups relocate to **indoor spaces** but **continue scheduled activities**. **Transportation & Busing** 

• **Small 15-20 passenger, air-conditioned buses**. 

• **Bus staff onboard** ensure safe, engaging commutes.

• **Centralized stops**: 

o **Manhattan (Upper West Side, Upper East Side, Battery Park, Astoria)**. o **Westchester, Bergen County, Montclair, Jersey City, Hoboken**. • **Bus routes adjust annually** for **optimal accessibility**. 

**CIT (Counselor-in-Training) Program** 

• Campers can remain **until age 16**. 

• **CIT program starts at 16**, but campers can **choose to stay one more summer as a  camper**. 

• **CITs work with younger groups** while remaining separate from older campers. 

**Q&A & Closing Remarks** 

• **No exceptions for less than 4 weeks** (even for international families). • **9th graders are welcome**, and many transfer from traditional camps. • **Bus stop information** is on the website, but routes adjust yearly. 

• Jason emphasized that The Nature Place is about **"how we do things, not just what  we do."** 

• The camp prioritizes **connection, personal growth, and a joyful, non-competitive  summer**.

title: Sportech Academy
text:
**Program Overview** 

Sport Tech Academy offers four distinct programs: 

**1. Soccer Program with A.C. Milan.** 

**2. Fashion Program with Raffles Institute.** 

**3. Design Program with Raffles Institute.** 

**4. Culinary Arts Program with Congusto Gourmet Institute.** 

All programs are hosted in Milan, leveraging the city’s status as a hub of sports, fashion,  and culinary excellence. Each program integrates lectures, practical workshops, and  cultural immersion. 

**1. Soccer Program with A.C. Milan** 

• **Age Range:** Boys and girls, ages **8–16.** 

• **Program Duration:** Last 2 weeks of June and first 2 weeks of July (with flexible  options for one to four weeks). 

• **Partnership Details:** 

o **Exclusive Access** to A.C. Milan training facilities: 

▪ **Youth Training Center:** The main site for most participants. 

▪ **First Team Training Center:** Reserved for a premium week (54 spots  only). 

o Program is designed in collaboration with A.C. Milan, featuring professional  coaches and structured training schedules. 

***Daily Routine and Activities*** 

• **Morning Training:** 9:00 AM to noon. 

• **Lunch and Rest:** 12:30 PM to 2:30 PM (includes relaxation and light activities). • **Afternoon Training:** 4:00 PM to 6:00 PM (focus on tournaments and friendly  matches). 

• **Evening:** Dinner, social activities (e.g., PlayStation, board games, sightseeing), and  relaxation.

***Cultural and Recreational Highlights*** 

• Weekend excursions to iconic locations: 

o **A.C. Milan Stadium and Museum.** 

o Milan city sightseeing. 

o Fun activities like visiting Zero Gravity (a trampoline park). 

***Accommodation and Supervision*** 

• Students stay in hotels with reserved floors for academy participants. o **Room Assignments:** Double occupancy. 

o **Safety:** 1:6 staff-to-student ratio, with 24/7 supervision. House parents  monitor students at all times. 

o **Meals:** 

▪ Breakfast and dinner in the hotel. 

▪ Lunch at the A.C. Milan training facility. 

***Key Features*** 

• Training sessions by **A.C. Milan coaches** with a focus on skill-building and  teamwork. 

• **Tournaments:** Friendly matches organized within the academy and against local  teams. 

• **Exclusive Merchandise:** Participants receive an official A.C. Milan kit (jersey,  socks, bag, and light jacket). 

***Cost*** 

• **€1,850 for one week** (residential). 

• **€3,500 for two weeks** (residential). 

**2. Fashion Program with Raffles Institute** 

• **Age Range:** High school students, ages **14–18.** 

• **Program Duration:** Four weeks in July. Students may join for one or two weeks.

***Program Highlights*** 

• Focuses on **fashion design, styling, and sustainability.** 

• Participants work on projects such as: 

o Styling and shooting models in professional studios. 

o Designing and creating sustainable garments (e.g., eco-friendly T-shirts). ***Structure*** 

• **Week 1 and 3:** Styling and Shooting. 

• **Week 2 and 4:** Tailoring and Sustainability. 

***Practical Experience*** 

• Conducted in Raffles Milan’s advanced laboratories and studios. • Students engage in **15 hours of theoretical learning** and **15 hours of hands-on  workshops** per week. 

• Final projects can be used for **portfolios** or college applications. ***Cultural Immersion*** 

• Excursions to: 

o **Fondazione Prada** (Prada Foundation). 

o **Armani Silos.** 

o **Lake Como.** 

**3. Design Program with Raffles Institute** 

• **Age Range:** High school students, ages **14–18.** 

• **Program Duration:** Four weeks in July (one or two-week modules). ***Program Highlights***
