• Two focus areas: 

o **Interior Design:** Reimagining spaces and working with AI tools. o **Product Design:** Developing creative concepts for everyday objects.

***Daily Structure*** 

• Morning lectures and afternoon labs in Raffles Milan’s facilities. • Evening activities include karaoke, movie nights, and socializing. 

***Cultural Immersion*** 

• Excursions to historical landmarks and cultural sites, similar to the Fashion  Program. 

**4. Culinary Arts Program with Congusto Gourmet Institute** 

• **Age Range:** High school students, ages **14–18**, and gap-year participants (18–19). • **Program Duration:** Four weeks in July (one or two-week modules). 

***Two Modules Offered*** 

**1. Italian Cooking:** 

a. Focus on pasta, regional dishes, and pizza-making. 

b. Includes a pizza and focaccia master class. 

**2. Italian Pastry:** 

a. Learn to make desserts, cookies, and pastries. 

***Additional Features*** 

• **Food Photography:** Learn to market culinary creations visually. • **Restaurant Business Basics:** Sessions on hospitality, customer service, and  managing a restaurant. 

• **Culinary Competition:** A Master Chef-style cooking contest. 

***Cultural Immersion*** 

• Visits to local markets for sourcing ingredients. 

***Cost*** 

• **€1,950 for one week** (residential).

• **€3,700 for two weeks** (residential). 

**Accommodation for Fashion, Design, and Culinary Programs** 

• **Location:** A Parto Dormitory, Milan. 

• **Features:** 

o Single rooms with private bathrooms. 

o Amenities include a gym, basketball court, cinema room, and 24/7 security. o Meals served in a cafeteria; lunch provided at respective program sites. 

**Future Plans** 

• Expanding year-round options, including: 

o Spring Break programs featuring soccer and culinary experiences. o Gap-year options for participants aged 18–19. 

• New locations planned for Lake Como and Rimini. 

**Key Questions and Answers** 

**1. Supervision:** 

a. 24/7 monitoring at accommodations and training centers. 

b. House parents and staff ensure safety and well-being. 

**2. Language Requirements:** 

a. Programs are conducted in English; no prior Italian knowledge is needed. **3. Cultural Integration:** 

a. Excursions to iconic Italian landmarks enhance the educational experience. **4. Air Conditioning:** 

a. All accommodations and program sites are fully air-conditioned.

title: SILC
text:
**Snapshot** 

SILC (often pronounced “Silk”) is a leadership-focused summer provider operating five boutique camps across Switzerland and France. The organization has run leadership programs for 14+ years, originally delivering 21st-century skills workshops in top international schools worldwide. 

Their camps center on real-life skill development — communication, teamwork, resilience, creativity, entrepreneurship — taught through adventure activities, corporate experiences, and daily leadership themes. SILC runs small, highly personalized programs (16–70 students max) with very diverse student bodies and a family-style culture. 

**Locations & Camp Portfolio** 

SILC operates **five camps**: 

**Switzerland (3 camps)** 

1\. Gstaad (ages 10–17) – classic Swiss summer camp with accessible adventure 2. Montreux (ages 14–17) – pre-university focus + high adventure 

3\. Leysin (ages 13–17) – outdoor survival + adventure challenges 

**France (2 camps)** 

4\. Corsica “Sea & Island Adventure” (ages 11–17) – catamaran-based island expedition 5. Cannes “Creative & Entrepreneurial Camp” (ages 13–17) – innovation, idea-building & coastal activities 

Each camp has its own theme and strengths, but all follow SILC’s leadership-skills DNA. 

**Group Size & Culture** 

SILC camps are intentionally small: 

● Largest camp = 70 students (Gstaad) 

● Montreux = ~40 students

● Leysin = ~30 students 

● Cannes = ~30 students 

● Corsica = 16–18 students (8 boys + 8 girls on two catamarans) 

Their boutique scale ensures strong supervision, low homesickness risk, and a supportive “family atmosphere.” Older students naturally mentor younger ones, reinforcing the values of care, contribution, and community. 

**Educational Philosophy & Daily Structure** 

The SILC model is built on **21st-century skills**, developed through **activities, not classrooms**. Skills include: 

● Goal-setting 

● Teamwork & communication 

● Decision-making 

● Conflict management 

● Creativity & entrepreneurship 

● Resilience & responsibility 

Each day includes: 

● A **leadership topic of the day** (e.g., goal-setting, communication) 

● A 10–15 minute leadership briefing 

● A full day of **outdoor activities and workshops** where students apply that skill ● Evening activities that reinforce reflection and community 

Their programs are extremely active — morning, afternoon, and evening are all scheduled, with many days fully off-campus in mountains, forests, oceans, or corporate environments.

**Camp-by-Camp Overview** 

**Gstaad (Switzerland)** 

● Ages 10–17 

● 60–70 students per session 

● Only camp offering 1-, 2-, 3-, or 4-week options 

● Most accessible entry point for first-time campers 

● High adventure but at a gentler level compared to other Swiss programs 

● Focus: interpersonal & intrapersonal skills (goal-setting, confidence, choices, communication) 

**Montreux — Swiss Summer Academy** 

● Ages 14–17 

● ~40 students 

● Located at the former Monte Rosa boarding school campus 

● Mixes pre-university content with adventure 

● Corporate visits: Nestlé, Hublot/Omega, UBS 

● Workshops: innovation, personal finance, branding, photography 

● Outdoor activities: glacier expedition with mountain-hut overnight, via ferrata, rock climbing, canyoning, boat building 

● Students even run their own gala evening 

● Very dynamic, academically enriching, and physically challenging **Leysin (Switzerland)**

● Ages 13–17 

● ~30 students 

● Strong outdoor and survival focus 

● More challenge-based and expedition-style than Gstaad 

● Blends the creativity of Montreux with the adventure of Swiss outdoor programs 

**Corsica — Sea & Island Adventure** 

● Ages 11–17 (sometimes exceptions for age 10) 

● 16–18 students total (two catamarans) 

● One session already sold out (high demand) 

● Island-circling expedition where boats are for travel, sleeping, and eating 

● Activities done on beaches, cliffs, forests, and ocean: snorkeling, hiking, paddling, exploration, island-based challenges 

● Leadership, resilience, and teamwork at the core 

● Includes an overnight camping experience on land 

● Very high adventure, extremely immersive 

**Cannes — Creative & Entrepreneurial Camp** 

● Ages 13–17 

● Located in a historic palace on the French Riviera 

● More leisure-oriented activities: beach days, biking, coastal excursions ● Core theme is creativity and entrepreneurship 

● Workshops: web design, photography, social media, brand creation, idea development

● Not an arts camp or a business bootcamp — more of a relaxed, exploratory environment for discovering talents 

**Safety, Staffing & Supervision** 

● High staff-to-student ratios across all camps 

● Gender-separated floors and same-gender staff assigned 

● Night watch staff on duty overnight 

● All technical activities run with professional instructors: 

○ Swiss mountain guides in Switzerland 

○ Certified French guides in France 

● SILC “trainers” (counselors) specialize in leadership development and personal coaching ● Strong pastoral culture built around belonging and personal growth 

**Languages & Nationalities** 

● Program language is **English**, across all camps (Switzerland + France) ● Students come from all over the world: 

○ USA, Latin America, Europe, Middle East 

○ Some Africa and Asia, but less dominant 

● Nationality cap of **~6% per country** to maintain diversity 

● Camps typically run full each summer, allowing them to control cohort balance

**Airports & Transfers** 

Airport transfers are **included** in the program fees. 

**Switzerland Camps:** 

● Geneva (preferred) 

● Zurich (possible but less common) 

**France Camps:** 

● Cannes: Nice Airport 

● Corsica: Figari Airport 

Families only need to arrange flights; everything else is covered. 

**Fees & What’s Included** 

SILC uses all-inclusive pricing. Example: 

● Cannes = €6,700 

● Tuition, lodging, insurance, transfers, all activities, guides, and supervision included ● No add-on activity fees 

All programs are 2-week sessions except Gstaad, which offers flexible durations. 

**Parent Consultation & Matching Process** 

SILC encourages advisors to introduce interested families directly. SILC will: ● Hold a 30–45 minute consultation

● Ask about interests, experience level, comfort with adventure, and goals ● Match the student to the most appropriate camp 

● Advisors may join these meetings or hand them off entirely 

They also offer post-camp follow-up and personal development plans for students who want continued growth. 

**Best-Fit Families** 

SILC is ideal for: 

● Families seeking a **small, international, boutique** camp experience ● Students who prefer structured, active days with constant engagement ● Kids who want to build leadership, confidence, and life skills 

● Teens interested in creativity, entrepreneurship, adventure, or pre-university insight ● Parents who value supervision, safety, and strong personal attention 

Less ideal for: 

● Students wanting a large, traditional, social American-style camp 

● Teens not comfortable with outdoor activities or active itineraries

title: Sangamon
text:
**Camp Sangamon** 

**Program Overview** 

Sangamon is a rustic, unplugged boys’ camp in Vermont built around outdoor living, free choice, and community. Cabins are intentionally simple, with no electricity or running water, encouraging kids to spend nearly all of their time outside and fully engaged with one another and the natural environment. The camp is completely screen-free, with no phones, video games, or media, which parents consistently see as a major benefit. 

**Philosophy & Camper Experience** 

Sangamon is rooted in the belief that there are many paths to success and that every camper belongs. The program emphasizes empathy, kindness, communication, and collaboration, with a strong focus on social-emotional growth. Campers are encouraged to practice conflict resolution, nonviolent communication, and community responsibility in a supportive, inclusive environment that celebrates individuality rather than competition. 

**Free Choice Activity Model** 

Campers operate within a true free choice system: they do not have to do any one activity, but they do need to be engaged in something. Activities are announced daily, and campers move fluidly between options based on interest and energy. A camper may spend an entire day immersed in woodshop or choose to rotate between sports, swimming, animal care, and creative pursuits. The emphasis is on agency, decision-making, and intrinsic motivation rather than schedules or required rotations. 

**Activities & Skill Development** 

Sangamon offers a wide range of hands-on activities, including woodworking, farming, horseback riding, archery, swimming, arts, crafts, sports, outdoor cooking, and nature exploration. While campers gain practical outdoor and creative skills, the primary goal is building independence, confidence, communication skills, and emotional awareness. Staff encourage campers to try new experiences when they are ready, rather than forcing participation. 

**Camper Fit** 

Sangamon is particularly well suited for boys who benefit from autonomy, variety, and movement. It works extremely well for campers with diverse interests, shorter attention spans, creative minds, or those who may struggle in rigid or highly structured environments. It is not an ideal fit for campers who require highly scheduled days, constant structure, or one-on-one support to function successfully. 

**Cabins, Supervision & Ratios** 

Camp operates with an exceptionally high staff-to-camper ratio of approximately 2:1. Cabins serve as each camper’s home base, where they sleep, eat meals, and check in daily with

counselors about plans and experiences. Staff members are both cabin counselors and activity leaders, ensuring consistent supervision and strong relationships throughout the day. 

**Community & Relationships** 

Strong relationships are central to the Sangamon experience. Campers form friendships across ages through shared interests rather than grade or age grouping. Cabin staff actively foster meaningful conversations, shared experiences, and reflection, helping campers develop deeper interpersonal skills. Nearly every camper forms a close connection with at least one adult mentor during their time at camp. 

**Sessions & Ages** 

Most new campers begin with a two-week session, which offers a manageable introduction and leaves campers eager to return. As campers grow older and return, many extend to longer sessions of three to six weeks. Younger campers typically attend shorter sessions, while returning and older campers often stay longer. 

**Health, Safety & Emotional Support** 

Sangamon maintains a well-staffed health center with RNs, EMTs, and access to local medical professionals, including pediatric and emergency care oversight. Medications are administered daily, and the camp regularly supports campers with allergies, medical needs, and emotional support requirements. Emotional well-being is treated proactively, with staff trained to identify and support homesickness, anxiety, and social challenges. 

**Food & Dietary Needs** 

The camp accommodates a range of dietary needs, including celiac and dairy-free diets, on a case-by-case basis. While Sangamon is not nut-free, families work closely with staff to ensure safe and appropriate meal planning. 

**Weekend Programming** 

Weekends offer a slightly different pace, including special breakfasts, staff-led seminars, themed all-camp games, and community events. Sundays include cabin cookouts and a full camp gathering focused on gratitude, reflection, and shared values. 

**Sister Camp Connection** 

Sangamon has a sister camp for girls, Betsy Cox, located next door. Campers connect weekly through socials, shared waterfront access, and occasional joint activities. Siblings have regular opportunities to see one another while still maintaining largely independent camp experiences. 

**Values & Culture** 

Sangamon emphasizes acceptance, empathy, and respect without framing these values politically or religiously. The camp acknowledges spirituality broadly through gratitude and reflection but does not offer religious instruction or services. Diversity, inclusion, and kindness are treated as core human values rather than ideological positions.

title: Robin Hood (NH) Zoom Recap
text:
**Camp Overview & Unique Features** 

**General Structure** 

• **One-session camp model** (No mid-season enrollments). 

• **Full summer experience:** 

o Majority stay for **full summer**, but ~25% leave after **four weeks**. 

o Those who leave do **not disrupt camp dynamics** (no second-session  arrivals). 

• **Long-standing traditions:** 

o Many staff members **grew up at camp**, including second-generation  campers. 

o A tight-knit, **family-like atmosphere**. 

**Camper Demographics & Environment** 

• **Midwestern influence:** The camp attracts many families from **Chicago and the  Midwest**, offering a **less status-driven, more inclusive environment** compared to  some East Coast camps. 

• **Diverse Population:** Campers from all over the **U.S. and internationally**, including  **London, Spain, Israel, Kuwait, Qatar**, and other countries. 

o Approximately **30-40 international campers** (around 10% of enrollment). o No more than **two non-English speakers per group** to encourage language  immersion. 

**Facilities & Investments** 

• **Significant facility upgrades** since COVID (multi-million-dollar investment). • **70-acre waterfront property** with expanded facilities. 

• **New structures & renovations:** 

o Dining hall **expansion** (increased capacity by 100). 

o New **two-story infirmary** on the lake (separate sick bays for boys & girls). o **Arts & crafts center relocation** and conversion of old space into boys’ bunk. o **Venice Beach-style weightlifting area**. 

o **6 tennis courts & 6 pickleball courts**.

**Program Structure & Approach** 

• **Focus on personal growth:** 

o Emphasis on **pleases and thank-yous, respect, and helping others**. o Campers return home as **better siblings, children, and citizens**. • **Structured Activity Selection:** 

o Activities are **chosen daily**, rather than signing up in advance. 

o Groups rotate together, **ensuring all campers try multiple activities**. o Campers **must try all activities** to encourage exposure and respect for  different experiences. 

• **No child walks alone:** 

o **Three or four counselors per group** accompany campers at all times. 

**Activities & Specialty Programs** 

**Sports & Instructional Improvements** 

• **New professional coaches** for major sports like **baseball, basketball, volleyball,  and tennis**. 

• **More experienced specialists** rather than former campers teaching major sports. • **Strong but balanced competition:** 

o **Not a “D1 athlete” training camp** but **competitive in regional**  

**tournaments**. 

o Girls’ teams are **undefeated**, and boys’ teams have won multiple  tournaments. 

**Arts, Music, & Theater** 

• **Expanded creative offerings:** 

o Theater & musicals introduced last year. 

o Increased staff specialization in **music and performing arts**. 

**Tripping & Outdoor Adventures** 

• **Excursions & Adventure Trips:** 

o Every camper **climbs a mountain** within the first three days.

o Multiple **optional hikes** throughout the summer. 

• **Changes to overnight excursions:** 

o **Older campers (9th-11th grade) have chaperoned trips with security**. o Younger campers go on **day-long excursions** but **return to camp at night** to  avoid safety risks. 

**Color War (5-day Event)** 

• **Highly immersive, all-camp competition.** 

• **Unique events:** 

o **Opening “break” is a major event** (last year’s wasn’t well received, aiming  to improve). 

o **Rubik’s Cube & chess tournaments** kick off the competition. 

o **Night 4 features a massive talent show**, including **dances from junior and  senior campers**. 

o Ends with a **sing-off and a tug-of-war**. 

• **Judging:** 

o Former **color war captains from past decades return to judge** (~90 judges  last year). 

**Enrollment & Marketing Challenges** 

**Current Enrollment** 

• **Capacity:** 400 campers. 

• **Remaining spots:** 50 open spots left. 

• **Strong word-of-mouth growth:** 

o **75% of new campers from referrals**. 

o Only **25% from camp consultants or brokers** (opposite of industry norms). **Outreach & Recruitment Issues** 

**1. Lack of Conversions from Referrals:** 

a. Last year, **only one camper enrolled from camp consultants** despite **60-70  leads**. 

b. Many **initial inquiries disappear without follow-up**.

**2. Aggressive Follow-Up Approach May Be Detrimental:** 

a. Current strategy: 

i. Immediate email after lead submission. 

ii. Second email **within 2-3 days**. 

iii. Phone call follow-ups. 

b. Consultant feedback: 

i. **Recommended waiting at least 24 hours** before initial contact. 

ii. **NYC & PA families prefer email** over unsolicited calls. 

iii. Personal introductions via **warm email intros** from camp consultants  work better. 

**Feedback & Adjustments** 

• **Less immediate outreach; more strategic follow-ups.** 

• **Stronger emphasis on softer, personal touches.** 

• **Leverage in-person visits & tours** (Woody willing to fly for family meetings). • **More visibility & engagement with camp consultants.** 

**Additional Camp Opportunities** 

**Rookie Day (Trial Experience)** 

• **One-day immersion for prospective campers & parents.** 

• Parents **stay on-site** while kids participate in activities with counselors. • **Key selling point:** Parents experience the **high-energy, communal dining hall  experience**. 

• **Date: July 13th, 2024**. 

**Gymnastics Expansion** 

• Current gymnastics program **is a weak point**. 

• **Plans to improve for 2024 & 2026.** 

• Potential **construction of a dedicated gymnastics pavilion** by 2026.

**Final Takeaways & Next Steps** 

**Strengths of Robin Hood Camp** 

• **Long-standing traditions & tight-knit alumni network.** • **Balanced focus on sports, arts, and outdoor adventure.** • **Single-session structure (no mid-summer disruption).** • **Diverse population from across the U.S. & internationally.** 

**Areas for Improvement** 

**1. Adjust recruitment follow-up strategy.** 

**a. More personalized, less aggressive outreach.** 

b. Focus on **email introductions over cold calls**.

title: Private_Prep_Recap
text:
Private Prep — Team Recap (Services, Strategy & How to Refer) 

Presenter & Partnership Notes 

Speaker: Steve Feldman (Founder). Longstanding partner to Camp Experts; known for proactive usage updates and transparent quarterly reporting for referred families. 

Footprint: Offices across NY Metro (NYC, Scarsdale, Port Washington, Ridgewood, Summit) and Bethesda, MD. ~70% of hours delivered online → serves students nationwide and internationally. 

What Private Prep Offers (4 Core Buckets) 

1) Test Prep — SAT/ACT for college; SSAT/ISEE for secondary admissions; SHSAT (NYC specialized HS), HSPT/Catholic HS tests. 2) College Admissions Guidance — Strategy from 8th–11th grade; school list building, app strategy, essays, ED/EA/RD plans. 3) Academic Support — Middle school through AP/advanced courses; writing, math, science, history. 4) Executive Functioning (EF) — Study skills, time management, organization, accountability. 

Typical on-ramp: Initial strategy consult → bespoke plan (align around camp, sports, theater calendars). 

Admissions Landscape & Strategy Signals for Families 

Selectivity is up. Most competitive schools use holistic review. Baseline bar = strong GPA in rigorous courses + competitive test scores; differentiators = activities, essays, recommendations, character. 

Course planning matters starting 8th–9th grade. PP balances student well‑being with rigor—no one-size-fits-all AP loading. 

Testing (SAT/ACT) — Current Reality & Timing 

Post‑COVID pendulum is swinging back toward testing at many competitive schools; families in PP/Camp Experts’ demographic typically benefit from submitting scores when strong. 

When to start: connect in late 10th grade; build plan over summer into junior year. Most students sit 2x (sometimes 3x; rarely 4x due to diminishing returns). PP runs full diagnostics for SAT & ACT to select one path. 

SAT is now digital and adaptive; ACT changes are in flux. PP guides policies on score choice/superscore per college list. 

Private Prep’s Test‑Prep Approach 

Three pillars: (1) Content mastery refresh (esp. grammar); (2) Strategy (timing, backsolving, elimination); (3) Performance prep (test‑day mindset). PP partners with

sports‑psych resources; emphasizes confidence and mental health as academic performance drivers. 

Executive Functioning (EF) — How It Works 

Goal: build durable habits for organization, planning and initiation; break long‑term work into weekly/daily action steps. 

Format: frequent, short touchpoints (e.g., 2–3x/week for 15–30 minutes). Coach = accountability partner (not Mom/Dad). Common targets: procrastination, planning backward from due dates, study schedules, emotional regulation. 

Outcome: lower family friction, higher follow‑through; skills translate to HS/college and early career. 

Operations, Communication & Quality Control 

Each lesson generates a written summary in PP’s platform → approved by a full‑time Director of Client Relations (DCR) and sent to parents. DCRs quarterback the relationship (student/parent ↔ tutor/coach), handle placement, and ensure alignment. 

Billing: summaries tie to payroll/billing; families billed bi‑weekly via card/ACH. 

Geography & Delivery 

Local presence in NY metro & DC; online delivery means PP supports students across the U.S. and abroad. International admissions experience: yes (e.g., Europe, Latin America). 

Pricing & Packages (for family expectation‑setting) 

Academic tutoring & Executive Functioning: ~$150–$250/hr (most experienced higher). Test Prep: ~$175–$400/hr. 

College Admissions: higher rates, typically packages (e.g., 20 or 40 hrs) to reserve counselor bandwidth: ~${7,000} for 20 hrs at $350/hr; ~${13,300}+ for 40 hrs (with ~5% package discount). Actual rates vary by coach. 

Many families begin with a lower‑commitment **Strategy Session**: roughly **$600–$1,500** to map a plan. 

Referral Tips for Camp Experts 

• Best initial touchpoint: Strategy Session (grades 8–10) or diagnostics (late 10th) for SAT/ACT plan. • Share calendar constraints (camp/sports), target colleges/majors, and specific struggles (e.g., procrastination) in your referral form—more context = better placement. • One family often becomes multi‑service, multi‑sibling → recurring referral value. 

Contacts

Primary: **Steve Feldman — steve@privateprep.com** Main line: **212‑244‑7190** (calls returned quickly). PP will route to the right Director based on location and bandwidth.
